[REASONING CONTEXT]
Amplify: closed_loop_cycle_detection; stability_assessment
Suppress: loop_blindness; reinforcing_loop_tolerance — do not assume that because prey start abundant, predators will keep growing. Track the feedback loop where more predators → fewer prey → predator decline.
[END REASONING CONTEXT]

## Step-by-step simulation

| | Prey (start) | After +50% growth | Predators hunt (×3 each) | Prey (after hunt) | Prey > 50? | Predators (after adjust) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Year 0 start** | 100 | — | — | — | — | 20 |
| **Year 1** | 100 | 150 | 20×3 = 60 eaten | **90** | Yes → +20% | **24** |
| **Year 2** | 90 | 135 | 24×3 = 72 eaten | **63** | Yes → +20% | **28.8** |
| **Year 3** | 63 | 94.5 | 28.8×3 = 86.4 eaten | **8.1** | No → −40% | **17.28** |
| **Year 4** | 8.1 | 12.15 | 17.28×3 = 51.84, but only 12.15 available → 0 | **0** | No → −40% | **10.368** |

## Answer: **No.** The predator population at the end of Year 4 is ~10.4, roughly **half** the starting 20.

The biologist's prediction is wrong. This is a classic feedback loop trap (exactly what the Suppress signal "reinforcing_loop_tolerance" warned against): predator growth in Years 1–2 is real, but the growing predator population overshoots the prey base in Year 3, crashing prey to 8.1. Once prey collapse, predators decline sharply for two consecutive years, ending well below where they started.
